Customer Churn Prediction¶

Context

AlphaCom, a leading telecommunications provider, has recently experienced a concerning rise in customer churn despite offering competitive services and a wide product portfolio. This increase is directly impacting revenue and undermining brand reputation in an intensely competitive market. Traditional retention strategies have proven inadequate because customer churn is influenced by a complex mix of factors, including service usage, billing preferences, contract types, and demographics. Without clear insights into these patterns, the company is left reacting to churn instead of preventing it.

Objective

As a data scientist at AlphaCom, you are tasked with developing a predictive model to identify customers at high risk of churn and uncover the key factors driving their decisions. Solving this problem will enable the company to proactively design targeted retention strategies, reduce churn-related losses, and improve customer lifetime value, ultimately safeguarding revenue and strengthening AlphaCom’s competitive position.

In [1]:
import warnings

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

# Libraries to help with reading and manipulating data
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# Library to split data
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

# libaries to help with data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Removes the limit for the number of displayed columns
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None)
# Sets the limit for the number of displayed rows
pd.set_option("display.max_rows", 100)


# Libraries different ensemble classifiers
from sklearn.ensemble import (
    BaggingRegressor,
    RandomForestRegressor,
    AdaBoostRegressor,
    GradientBoostingRegressor,
    StackingRegressor,
)

from xgboost import XGBRegressor
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeRegressor

# Libraries to get different metric scores
from sklearn import metrics
from sklearn.metrics import (
    confusion_matrix,
    accuracy_score,
    precision_score,
    recall_score,
    f1_score,
)

# To tune different models
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

sc = StandardScaler()

Loading Data

In [2]:
Customer_Churn = pd.read_csv("/content/customer_churn.csv")
In [3]:
Data = Customer_Churn.copy()

Overview

In [4]:
Data.head()

3 Male 0 No No 45.0 No No phone service DSL Yes No Yes Yes No No One year No bank transfer (automatic) $42.3 $1840.75 No
4 Female 0 No No 2.0 Yes No Fiber optic No No No No No No Month-to-month Yes ELECTRONIC CHECK $70.7 $nan yes

Shape of the Data

In [5]:
print(f"There are {Data.shape[0]} rows and {Data.shape[1]} columns.")

There are 12055 rows and 20 columns.

Data types of all columns

In [6]:
Data.info()

 #   Column            Non-Null Count  Dtype

dtypes: float64(1), int64(1), object(18)
memory usage: 1.8+ MB

Monthly charges and Total charges are showing object type but it should be float type and Churn is also showing object type but it should be binary type.

In [7]:
Data.Churn.unique()
Out[7]:
array(['  No  ', 'NO', 'YES', 'yes', 'Yes', 'No', 'no', '  Yes  '],
      dtype=object)
In [8]:
Data['Churn'] = Data['Churn'].astype(str).str.lower().str.strip().replace({'yes': 1,'no': 0})
In [9]:
dollar_prices = Data[Data['MonthlyCharges'].str.contains(r'\$', na=False)]['MonthlyCharges'].reset_index(drop=True)
pound_prices  = Data[Data['MonthlyCharges'].str.contains(r'£', na=False)]['MonthlyCharges'].reset_index(drop=True)
price_table = pd.concat([dollar_prices, pound_prices], axis=1)
price_table.columns = ['Dollar ($)', 'Pound (£)']
price_table
Out[9]:
Dollar ($) Pound (£)
0 $29.85 £49.05
1 $56.95 £25.7
2 $53.85 £69.5
3 $42.3 £101.9
4 $70.7 £84.2
... ... ...
11866 $89.5 NaN
11867 $74.7 NaN
11868 $113.4 NaN
11869 $99.65 NaN
11870 $100.05 NaN

11871 rows × 2 columns

The significant difference between the number of entries with '$' (11871 rows) and '£' (183 rows) in 'MonthlyCharges' indicates a likely data entry error. So we will assume everything is in Dollar and proceed.

In [10]:
dollar_prices = Data[Data['TotalCharges'].str.contains(r'\$', na=False)]['TotalCharges'].reset_index(drop=True)
pound_prices  = Data[Data['TotalCharges'].str.contains(r'£', na=False)]['TotalCharges'].reset_index(drop=True)
price_table = pd.concat([dollar_prices, pound_prices], axis=1)
price_table.columns = ['Dollar ($)', 'Pound (£)']
price_table
Out[10]:
Dollar ($) Pound (£)
0 $29.85 £49.05
1 $1889.5 £1188.2
2 $108.15 £1071.4
3 $1840.75 £2681.15
4 $nan £nan
... ... ...
11866 $573.3 NaN
11867 $nan NaN
11868 $8164.1 NaN
11869 $5497.05 NaN
11870 $6034.85 NaN

11871 rows × 2 columns

The significant difference between the number of entries with '$' (11871 rows) and '£' (183 rows) in 'TotalCharges' indicates a likely data entry error. So we will assume everything is in Dollar and proceed.

Correcting data types

In [11]:
Data['MonthlyCharges'] = Data['MonthlyCharges'].apply(lambda X: X.replace("$",""))
Data['MonthlyCharges'] = Data['MonthlyCharges'].apply(lambda X: X.replace("£",""))
Data['MonthlyCharges'] = Data['MonthlyCharges'].astype("float")
Data['TotalCharges'] = Data['TotalCharges'].apply(lambda X: X.replace("$",""))
Data['TotalCharges'] = Data['TotalCharges'].apply(lambda X: X.replace("£",""))
Data['TotalCharges'] = Data['TotalCharges'].astype("float")
In [12]:
Data.info()

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>

Data columns (total 20 columns):
 #   Column            Non-Null Count  Dtype  
---  ------            --------------  -----

 1   SeniorCitizen     12055 non-null  int64

 3   Dependents        12055 non-null  object 
 4   tenure            11451 non-null  float64

In [13]:
Data.tenure.unique()
Out[13]:
array([ 1., 34.,  2., 45.,  8., 22., 10., 28., 62., 13., 16., 58., 49.,
       25., 69., 52., 71., 21., nan, 12., 30., 72., 17., 27.,  5., 46.,
       11., 70., 63., 43., 15., 60., 18., 66., 47.,  9.,  3., 31., 50.,
       64., 56.,  7., 42., 35., 48., 29., 65., 38., 68., 32., 55., 37.,
       36., 41.,  6.,  4., 33., 67., 23., 57., 61., 14., 20., 53., 40.,
       59., 24., 44., 19., 54., 51., 26.,  0., 39., -1., 73., -2., 74.,
       -3.])

The 'tenure' column contains negative values, which are likely data entry errors and can be problematic for analysis. To address this, these erroneous values will be replaced with NaN (Not a Number) and then imputed with the median of the 'tenure' column. This approach maintains data robustness by using a central tendency measure, minimizing the impact of outliers or incorrect entries.

In [14]:
Data.loc[Data['tenure'] < 0, 'tenure'] = np.nan
Data['tenure'].fillna(Data['tenure'].median(), inplace=True)

Lets fill missing values of monthly charges and total charges with median of respective column.

In [16]:
Data['MonthlyCharges'].fillna(Data['MonthlyCharges'].median(), inplace=True)
Data['TotalCharges'].fillna(Data['TotalCharges'].median(), inplace=True)

As we can see, there are still a few records in the 'TotalCharges' column that are negative. Let's correct this by replacing these negative values with NaN and then filling them with the median of the column.

In [22]:
Data.loc[Data['TotalCharges'] < 0, 'TotalCharges'] = np.nan
Data['TotalCharges'].fillna(Data['TotalCharges'].median(), inplace=True)

There are some unwanted spaces in the 'PaymentMethod' column. We will remove that.

In [25]:
Data['PaymentMethod'] = Data['PaymentMethod'].astype(str).str.lower().str.strip()

Now, the data appears to be thoroughly cleaned and consistent across all columns. We can confidently proceed with the Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) phase.

Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Univariate Analysis¶

1. Distribution of 'Churn'

In [28]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
ax = sns.countplot(x='Churn', data=Data, palette='viridis')
plt.title('Distribution of Churn (0: No Churn, 1: Churn)')
plt.xlabel('Churn')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.xticks(ticks=[0, 1], labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])

total = len(Data['Churn'])
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * p.get_height()/total)
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.show()
  • Approximately 28% of the customers have churned (represented by '1'), while about 72% have not churned (represented by '0').

2. Distribution of 'gender'

In [29]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='gender', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Distribution of Gender')
plt.xlabel('Gender')
plt.ylabel('Count')

total = len(Data['gender'])
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * p.get_height()/total)
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.show()
  • The gender distribution is nearly balanced, with slightly more males (55.7%) than females (44.3%) in the dataset.

3. Distribution of 'SeniorCitizen'

In [30]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='SeniorCitizen', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Distribution of Senior Citizen')
plt.xlabel('SeniorCitizen (0: No, 1: Yes)')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.xticks(ticks=[0, 1], labels=['No', 'Yes'])

total = len(Data['SeniorCitizen'])
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * p.get_height()/total)
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.show()
  • The plot indicates that the vast majority of customers (88.0%) are not senior citizens, while only a small percentage (12.0%) are senior citizen.

4. Distribution of 'Partner'

In [31]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='Partner', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Distribution of Partner')
plt.xlabel('Partner')
plt.ylabel('Count')

total = len(Data['Partner'])
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * p.get_height()/total)
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.show()
  • The plot shows that the majority of customers (58.0%) do not have a partner, while 42.0% do.

5. Distribution of 'Dependents'

In [32]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='Dependents', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Distribution of Dependents')
plt.xlabel('Dependents')
plt.ylabel('Count')

total = len(Data['Dependents'])
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * p.get_height()/total)
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.show()
  • The plot shows that the majority of customers (73.4%) do not have dependents, while 26.6% do.

6. Distribution of 'tenure'

In [33]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
sns.histplot(Data['tenure'], bins=30, kde=True, palette='viridis')
plt.title('Distribution of Customer Tenure')
plt.xlabel('Tenure (Months)')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.show()
  • The distribution of 'tenure' shows a bimodal shape, with a significant number of customers having very short tenure (around 0-5 months) and another peak for customers with longer tenure (around 65-70 months). This suggests that many customers either churn early or stay for a long time.

7. Distribution of 'PhoneService'

In [34]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='PhoneService', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Distribution of PhoneService')
plt.xlabel('PhoneService')
plt.ylabel('Count')

total = len(Data['PhoneService'])
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * p.get_height()/total)
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.show()
  • A significant majority of customers (89.1%) have phone service, while only (10.9%) do not.

8. Distribution of 'MultipleLines'

In [35]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='MultipleLines', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Distribution of MultipleLines')
plt.xlabel('MultipleLines')
plt.ylabel('Count')

total = len(Data['MultipleLines'])
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * p.get_height()/total)
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.show()
  • The plot shows that the 'No' category for multiple lines (referring to no multiple lines for those with phone service) is the largest, followed by 'Yes' and 'No phone service'. This indicates that a significant portion of customers do not have multiple lines, or do not have phone service at all.

9. Distribution of 'InternetService'

In [36]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='InternetService', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Distribution of Internet Service')
plt.xlabel('Internet Service')
plt.ylabel('Count')

total = len(Data['InternetService'])
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * p.get_height()/total)
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.show()
  • The 'Fiber optic' service has the highest number of customers, followed by 'DSL', and then a significant portion of customers with 'No' internet service.

10. Distribution of 'OnlineSecurity'

In [37]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='OnlineSecurity', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Distribution of Online Security')
plt.xlabel('Online Security')
plt.ylabel('Count')

total = len(Data['OnlineSecurity'])
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * p.get_height()/total)
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.show()
  • The 'No' category has the highest count, followed by 'Yes', and then 'No internet service'. This indicates that a large portion of customers do not have online security or do not have internet service at all.

11. Distribution of 'OnlineBackup'

In [38]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='OnlineBackup', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Distribution of Online Backup')
plt.xlabel('Online Backup')
plt.ylabel('Count')

total = len(Data['OnlineBackup'])
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * p.get_height()/total)
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.show()
  • The 'No' category has the highest count, followed by 'Yes', and then 'No internet service'. This indicates that a large portion of customers do not have online backup or do not have internet service at all.

plt.ylabel('Count')

    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.show()
  • The 'Yes' category has the highest count, followed by 'No', and then 'No internet service'. This suggests that a considerable number of customers do not have device protection or do not have internet service at all.

In [40]:

plt.title('Distribution of Tech Support')
plt.xlabel('Tech Support')
plt.ylabel('Count')

total = len(Data['TechSupport'])
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * p.get_height()/total)
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.show()
  • The 'No' category has the highest count, followed by 'Yes', and then 'No internet service'. This implies that a substantial number of customers either do not have technical support or do not have internet service at all.

plt.title('Distribution of Streaming TV')
plt.xlabel('Streaming TV')
plt.ylabel('Count')

total = len(Data['StreamingTV'])
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * p.get_height()/total)
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.show()
  • The distribution of 'StreamingTV' shows that the 'No' category has the highest count, followed by 'Yes', and then 'No internet service'. This suggests that a significant portion of customers do not subscribe to streaming TV services or do not have internet service at all.

plt.title('Distribution of Streaming Movies')

for p in ax.patches:
