    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.show()
  • The distribution of 'StreamingMovies' shows that the 'No' category has the highest count, followed by 'Yes', and then 'No internet service'. This suggests that a significant portion of customers do not subscribe to streaming movie services or do not have internet service at all.

16. Distribution of 'Contract'

In [43]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='Contract', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Distribution of Contract Type')
plt.xlabel('Contract Type')
plt.ylabel('Count')

total = len(Data['Contract'])
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * p.get_height()/total)
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.show()
  • The distribution of 'Contract' types shows that 'Month-to-month' contracts are the most prevalent, followed by 'Two year' and then 'One year' contracts. This indicates that a significant portion of customers prefer shorter, more flexible contract durations.

17. Distribution of 'PaperlessBilling'

In [44]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='PaperlessBilling', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Distribution of Paperless Billing')
plt.xlabel('Paperless Billing')
plt.ylabel('Count')

total = len(Data['PaperlessBilling'])
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * p.get_height()/total)
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.show()
  • The plot shows that a higher number of customers (51.1%) have opted for paperless billing, while 48.9% have not.

18. Distribution of 'PaymentMethod'

In [45]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='PaymentMethod', palette='viridis', order=Data['PaymentMethod'].value_counts().index)
plt.title('Distribution of Payment Method')
plt.xlabel('Payment Method')
plt.ylabel('Count')

total = len(Data['PaymentMethod'])
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * p.get_height()/total)
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha='right')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
  • The 'electronic check' is the most common payment method, followed by 'mailed check', 'bank transfer (automatic)', and 'credit card (automatic)'.

19. Distribution of 'MonthlyCharges'

In [46]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
sns.histplot(Data['MonthlyCharges'], bins=30, kde=True, palette='viridis')
plt.title('Distribution of Monthly Charges')
plt.xlabel('Monthly Charges ($)')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.show()
  • we can observe that there's a bimodal distribution. There's a significant peak at lower monthly charges (around 20 Dollar) and another broader peak ranging from 70 Dollar to 100 Dollar. This suggests that customers tend to either opt for very basic, low-cost plans or more comprehensive, higher-cost packages. Very few customers fall into the mid-range of monthly charges.

20. Distribution of 'TotalCharges'

In [47]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
sns.histplot(Data['TotalCharges'], bins=30, kde=True, palette='viridis')
plt.title('Distribution of Total Charges')
plt.xlabel('Total Charges ($)')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.show()
  • we can see a right-skewed distribution, indicating that a large number of customers have lower total charges, while fewer customers accumulate very high total charges.

Bivariate Analysis¶

1. Churn by Gender

In [48]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='gender', hue='Churn', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Churn by Gender')
plt.xlabel('Gender')
plt.ylabel('Count')

total_gender_counts = Data['gender'].value_counts()

for p in ax.patches:
    height = p.get_height()
    gender_pos = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2

    # Determine which gender this patch corresponds to based on x-position
    if gender_pos < 0.5:  # Assuming 'Female' is at x=0, 'Male' at x=1
        total = total_gender_counts.get('Female', 0)
    else:
        total = total_gender_counts.get('Male', 0)

    if total > 0:
        percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * height / total)
        ax.annotate(percentage, (gender_pos, height), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • The plot shows that both males and females have a similar churn rate, around 28%. This suggests that gender alone might not be a significant factor in predicting customer churn.

2. Churn by Senior Citizen

In [49]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='SeniorCitizen', hue='Churn', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Churn by Senior Citizen')
plt.xlabel('Senior Citizen (0: No, 1: Yes)')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.xticks(ticks=[0, 1], labels=['No', 'Yes'])

total_seniorcitizen_counts = Data['SeniorCitizen'].value_counts()

for p in ax.patches:
    height = p.get_height()
    senior_citizen_status = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2

    if senior_citizen_status < 0.5:  # Assuming 'No' is at x=0
        total = total_seniorcitizen_counts.get(0, 0)
    else:  # Assuming 'Yes' is at x=1
        total = total_seniorcitizen_counts.get(1, 0)

    if total > 0:
        percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * height / total)
        ax.annotate(percentage, (p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2., height), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • The plot reveals that senior citizens have a higher churn rate compared to non-senior citizens. This suggests that being a senior citizen might be a significant factor influencing churn.

3. Churn by Partner Status

In [50]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='Partner', hue='Churn', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Churn by Partner Status')
plt.xlabel('Partner')
plt.ylabel('Count')

total_partner_counts = Data['Partner'].value_counts()

for p in ax.patches:
    height = p.get_height()
    partner_status = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2

    if partner_status < 0.5:  # Assuming 'No' is at x=0
        total = total_partner_counts.get('No', 0)
    else:  # Assuming 'Yes' is at x=1
        total = total_partner_counts.get('Yes', 0)

    if total > 0:
        percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * height / total)
        ax.annotate(percentage, (p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2., height), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • The customers without a partner churn at a higher rate than those with a partner, suggesting that having a partner might indeed contribute to customer loyalty.

4. Churn by Dependents Status

In [51]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='Dependents', hue='Churn', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Churn by Dependents Status')
plt.xlabel('Dependents')
plt.ylabel('Count')

total_dependents_counts = Data['Dependents'].value_counts()

for p in ax.patches:
    height = p.get_height()
    dependents_status = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2

    if dependents_status < 0.5:  # Assuming 'No' is at x=0
        total = total_dependents_counts.get('No', 0)
    else:  # Assuming 'Yes' is at x=1
        total = total_dependents_counts.get('Yes', 0)

    if total > 0:
        percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * height / total)
        ax.annotate(percentage, (p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2., height), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • The plot shows that customers who do not have dependents have a significantly higher churn rate compared to those who do have dependents. This indicates that customers with dependents are more likely to remain with the service, suggesting that dependents may be a factor in customer retention.

5. Churn by Tenure

In [52]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.histplot(data=Data, x='tenure', hue='Churn', multiple='stack', palette='viridis', kde=True)
plt.title('Churn by Tenure')
plt.xlabel('Tenure (Months)')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • The histogram shows that a significant portion of churn occurs among customers with very short tenures (0-5 months). As tenure increases, the proportion of churned customers generally decreases, and customers with longer tenures (60-70 months) show a much lower churn rate. This indicates that newer customers are more prone to churn, while long-term customers are more loyal.

6. Churn by PhoneService

In [53]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='PhoneService', hue='Churn', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Churn by PhoneService')
plt.xlabel('PhoneService')
plt.ylabel('Count')

total_phoneservice_counts = Data['PhoneService'].value_counts()

for p in ax.patches:
    height = p.get_height()
    phoneservice_status = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2

    if phoneservice_status < 0.5:  # Assuming 'No' is at x=0
        total = total_phoneservice_counts.get('No', 0)
    else:  # Assuming 'Yes' is at x=1
        total = total_phoneservice_counts.get('Yes', 0)

    if total > 0:
        percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * height / total)
        ax.annotate(percentage, (p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2., height), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • The plot indicates that the presence or absence of phone service does not significantly differentiate churn rates.

7. Churn by MultipleLines

In [54]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='MultipleLines', hue='Churn', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Churn by MultipleLines')
plt.xlabel('MultipleLines')
plt.ylabel('Count')

# Pre-calculate churn percentages from the Data DataFrame
churn_percentages = Data.groupby('MultipleLines')['Churn'].value_counts(normalize=True).unstack()

# Get the x-tick labels and their positions to map patch x-coordinates to category names
x_tick_positions = ax.get_xticks()
x_tick_labels = [label.get_text() for label in ax.get_xticklabels()]

# Assuming ax.containers[0] holds 'No Churn' bars and ax.containers[1] holds 'Churn' bars
if len(ax.containers) > 1:
    # Iterate over both containers (No Churn and Churn)
    for container_idx, container in enumerate(ax.containers):
        for patch_idx, patch in enumerate(container):
            # Calculate the center x-coordinate of the bar
            bar_center_x = patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2

            # Find the corresponding category label based on the x-coordinate
            # This requires finding the closest x-tick position
            closest_tick_idx = (np.abs(x_tick_positions - bar_center_x)).argmin()
            current_category = x_tick_labels[closest_tick_idx]

            # Get the correct percentage based on container_idx (0 for No Churn, 1 for Churn)
            if current_category in churn_percentages.index:
                percentage_val = churn_percentages.loc[current_category, container_idx] * 100
                ax.annotate(f'{percentage_val:.1f}%',
                            (patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2., patch.get_height()),
                            ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10)

plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()

  • Customers with 'Yes' (multiple lines) have the highest churn rate at approximately 30.3%.

  • Customers with 'No' (single phone line) have a churn rate of about 26.9%.

  • Customers with 'No phone service' have the lowest churn rate at around 24.7%.

8. Churn by InternetService

In [55]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='InternetService', hue='Churn', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Churn by Internet Service')
plt.xlabel('Internet Service')
plt.ylabel('Count')

# Pre-calculate churn percentages
churn_percentages = Data.groupby('InternetService')['Churn'].value_counts(normalize=True).unstack()

# Get the x-tick labels and their positions to map patch x-coordinates to category names
x_tick_positions = ax.get_xticks()
x_tick_labels = [label.get_text() for label in ax.get_xticklabels()]

# Assuming ax.containers[0] holds 'No Churn' bars and ax.containers[1] holds 'Churn' bars
if len(ax.containers) > 1:
    # Iterate over both containers (No Churn and Churn)
    for container_idx, container in enumerate(ax.containers):
        for patch in container:
            # Calculate the center x-coordinate of the bar
            bar_center_x = patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2

            # Find the corresponding category label based on the x-coordinate
            # This requires finding the closest x-tick position
            closest_tick_idx = (np.abs(x_tick_positions - bar_center_x)).argmin()
            current_category = x_tick_labels[closest_tick_idx]

            # Get the correct percentage based on container_idx (0 for No Churn, 1 for Churn)
            if current_category in churn_percentages.index:
                percentage_val = churn_percentages.loc[current_category, container_idx] * 100
                ax.annotate(f'{percentage_val:.1f}%',
                            (patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2., patch.get_height()),
                            ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10)

plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • Fiber optic internet service has the highest churn rate at approximately 44.9%. This indicates that almost half of the customers using fiber optic churn.
  • DSL internet service has a significantly lower churn rate of about 21.7%.
  • Customers with No internet service have the lowest churn rate at approximately 9.5%.

This analysis clearly shows that customers with 'Fiber optic' internet service are far more likely to churn than those with 'DSL' or no internet service.

9. Churn by OnlineSecurity

In [56]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='OnlineSecurity', hue='Churn', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Churn by Online Security')
plt.xlabel('Online Security')
plt.ylabel('Count')

# Pre-calculate churn percentages from the Data DataFrame
churn_percentages = Data.groupby('OnlineSecurity')['Churn'].value_counts(normalize=True).unstack()

# Get the x-tick labels and their positions to map patch x-coordinates to category names
x_tick_positions = ax.get_xticks()
x_tick_labels = [label.get_text() for label in ax.get_xticklabels()]

# Assuming ax.containers[0] holds 'No Churn' bars and ax.containers[1] holds 'Churn' bars
if len(ax.containers) > 1:
    # Iterate over both containers (No Churn and Churn)
    for container_idx, container in enumerate(ax.containers):
        for patch in container:
            # Calculate the center x-coordinate of the bar
            bar_center_x = patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2

            # Find the corresponding category label based on the x-coordinate
            # This requires finding the closest x-tick position
            closest_tick_idx = (np.abs(x_tick_positions - bar_center_x)).argmin()
            current_category = x_tick_labels[closest_tick_idx]

            # Get the correct percentage based on container_idx (0 for No Churn, 1 for Churn)
            if current_category in churn_percentages.index:
                percentage_val = churn_percentages.loc[current_category, container_idx] * 100
                ax.annotate(f'{percentage_val:.1f}%',
                            (patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2., patch.get_height()),
                            ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10)

plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • Customers with 'No Online Security' have a significantly higher churn rate of approximately 43.1%.
  • Customers with 'Yes Online Security' have a much lower churn rate of about 14.4%.
  • Customers with 'No internet service' have the lowest churn rate at approximately 9.3%.

This indicates that the presence of online security is a strong factor in customer retention, with customers lacking this service being more prone to churn. Customers who do not have internet service at all are the least likely to churn.

10. Churn by OnlineBackup

In [57]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='OnlineBackup', hue='Churn', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Churn by Online Backup')
plt.xlabel('Online Backup')
plt.ylabel('Count')

# Pre-calculate churn percentages from the Data DataFrame
churn_percentages = Data.groupby('OnlineBackup')['Churn'].value_counts(normalize=True).unstack()

# Get the x-tick labels and their positions to map patch x-coordinates to category names
x_tick_positions = ax.get_xticks()
x_tick_labels = [label.get_text() for label in ax.get_xticklabels()]

# Assuming ax.containers[0] holds 'No Churn' bars and ax.containers[1] holds 'Churn' bars
if len(ax.containers) > 1:
    # Iterate over both containers (No Churn and Churn)
    for container_idx, container in enumerate(ax.containers):
        for patch in container:
            # Calculate the center x-coordinate of the bar
            bar_center_x = patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2

            # Find the corresponding category label based on the x-coordinate
            # This requires finding the closest x-tick position
            closest_tick_idx = (np.abs(x_tick_positions - bar_center_x)).argmin()
            current_category = x_tick_labels[closest_tick_idx]

            # Get the correct percentage based on container_idx (0 for No Churn, 1 for Churn)
            if current_category in churn_percentages.index:
                percentage_val = churn_percentages.loc[current_category, container_idx] * 100
                ax.annotate(f'{percentage_val:.1f}%',
                            (patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2., patch.get_height()),
                            ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10)

plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • Customers with 'No Online Backup' have a significantly higher churn rate of approximately 40.4%.
  • Customers with 'Yes Online Backup' have a much lower churn rate of about 22.2%.
  • Customers with 'No internet service' have the lowest churn rate at approximately 9.3%.

This suggests that offering online backup services might contribute to customer retention, as customers without it are more likely to churn.

11. Churn by DeviceProtection

In [58]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='DeviceProtection', hue='Churn', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Churn by Device Protection')
plt.xlabel('Device Protection')
plt.ylabel('Count')

# Pre-calculate churn percentages from the Data DataFrame
churn_percentages = Data.groupby('DeviceProtection')['Churn'].value_counts(normalize=True).unstack()

# Get the x-tick labels and their positions to map patch x-coordinates to category names
x_tick_positions = ax.get_xticks()
x_tick_labels = [label.get_text() for label in ax.get_xticklabels()]

# Assuming ax.containers[0] holds 'No Churn' bars and ax.containers[1] holds 'Churn' bars
if len(ax.containers) > 1:
    # Iterate over both containers (No Churn and Churn)
    for container_idx, container in enumerate(ax.containers):
        for patch in container:
            # Calculate the center x-coordinate of the bar
            bar_center_x = patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2

            # Find the corresponding category label based on the x-coordinate
            # This requires finding the closest x-tick position
            closest_tick_idx = (np.abs(x_tick_positions - bar_center_x)).argmin()
            current_category = x_tick_labels[closest_tick_idx]

            # Get the correct percentage based on container_idx (0 for No Churn, 1 for Churn)
            if current_category in churn_percentages.index:
                percentage_val = churn_percentages.loc[current_category, container_idx] * 100
                ax.annotate(f'{percentage_val:.1f}%',
                            (patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2., patch.get_height()),
                            ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10)

plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • Customers without 'Device Protection' have a significantly higher churn rate of approximately 44%.
  • Customers with 'Device Protection' have a much lower churn rate of about 24.1%.
  • Customers with 'No internet service' have the lowest churn rate at approximately 9.5%.

This indicates that providing device protection services might contribute to customer retention, as customers without it are more likely to churn.

12. Churn by TechSupport

In [59]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='TechSupport', hue='Churn', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Churn by Tech Support')
plt.xlabel('Tech Support')
plt.ylabel('Count')

# Pre-calculate churn percentages from the Data DataFrame
churn_percentages = Data.groupby('TechSupport')['Churn'].value_counts(normalize=True).unstack()

# Get the x-tick labels and their positions to map patch x-coordinates to category names
x_tick_positions = ax.get_xticks()
x_tick_labels = [label.get_text() for label in ax.get_xticklabels()]

# Assuming ax.containers[0] holds 'No Churn' bars and ax.containers[1] holds 'Churn' bars
if len(ax.containers) > 1:
    # Iterate over both containers (No Churn and Churn)
    for container_idx, container in enumerate(ax.containers):
        for patch in container:
            # Calculate the center x-coordinate of the bar
            bar_center_x = patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2

            # Find the corresponding category label based on the x-coordinate
            # This requires finding the closest x-tick position
            closest_tick_idx = (np.abs(x_tick_positions - bar_center_x)).argmin()
            current_category = x_tick_labels[closest_tick_idx]

            # Get the correct percentage based on container_idx (0 for No Churn, 1 for Churn)
            if current_category in churn_percentages.index:
                percentage_val = churn_percentages.loc[current_category, container_idx] * 100
                ax.annotate(f'{percentage_val:.1f}%',
                            (patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2., patch.get_height()),
                            ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10)

plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • Customers with 'No Tech Support' have a significantly higher churn rate of approximately 44.2%.
  • Customers with 'Yes Tech Support' have a much lower churn rate of about 12.8%.
  • Customers with 'No internet service' have the lowest churn rate at approximately 9.4%.

This highlights the importance of technical support in customer retention, as customers without it are considerably more likely to churn.

13. Churn by StreamingTV

In [60]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='StreamingTV', hue='Churn', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Churn by Streaming TV')
plt.xlabel('Streaming TV')
plt.ylabel('Count')

# Pre-calculate churn percentages from the Data DataFrame
churn_percentages = Data.groupby('StreamingTV')['Churn'].value_counts(normalize=True).unstack()

# Get the x-tick labels and their positions to map patch x-coordinates to category names
x_tick_positions = ax.get_xticks()
x_tick_labels = [label.get_text() for label in ax.get_xticklabels()]

# Assuming ax.containers[0] holds 'No Churn' bars and ax.containers[1] holds 'Churn' bars
if len(ax.containers) > 1:
    # Iterate over both containers (No Churn and Churn)
    for container_idx, container in enumerate(ax.containers):
        for patch in container:
            # Calculate the center x-coordinate of the bar
            bar_center_x = patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2

            # Find the corresponding category label based on the x-coordinate
            # This requires finding the closest x-tick position
            closest_tick_idx = (np.abs(x_tick_positions - bar_center_x)).argmin()
            current_category = x_tick_labels[closest_tick_idx]

            # Get the correct percentage based on container_idx (0 for No Churn, 1 for Churn)
            if current_category in churn_percentages.index:
                percentage_val = churn_percentages.loc[current_category, container_idx] * 100
                ax.annotate(f'{percentage_val:.1f}%',
                            (patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2., patch.get_height()),
                            ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10)

plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • Customers with 'No Streaming TV' have a higher churn rate of approximately 40.0%.
  • Customers with 'Yes Streaming TV' have a slightly lower churn rate of about 26.9%.
  • Customers with 'No internet service' have the lowest churn rate at approximately 9.3%.

This indicates that customers without streaming TV are somewhat more likely to churn than those with it.

14. Churn by StreamingMovies

In [61]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='StreamingMovies', hue='Churn', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Churn by Streaming Movies')
plt.xlabel('Streaming Movies')
plt.ylabel('Count')

# Pre-calculate churn percentages from the Data DataFrame
churn_percentages = Data.groupby('StreamingMovies')['Churn'].value_counts(normalize=True).unstack()

# Get the x-tick labels and their positions to map patch x-coordinates to category names
x_tick_positions = ax.get_xticks()
x_tick_labels = [label.get_text() for label in ax.get_xticklabels()]

# Assuming ax.containers[0] holds 'No Churn' bars and ax.containers[1] holds 'Churn' bars
if len(ax.containers) > 1:
    # Iterate over both containers (No Churn and Churn)
    for container_idx, container in enumerate(ax.containers):
        for patch in container:
            # Calculate the center x-coordinate of the bar
            bar_center_x = patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2

            # Find the corresponding category label based on the x-coordinate
            # This requires finding the closest x-tick position
            closest_tick_idx = (np.abs(x_tick_positions - bar_center_x)).argmin()
            current_category = x_tick_labels[closest_tick_idx]

            # Get the correct percentage based on container_idx (0 for No Churn, 1 for Churn)
            if current_category in churn_percentages.index:
                percentage_val = churn_percentages.loc[current_category, container_idx] * 100
                ax.annotate(f'{percentage_val:.1f}%',
                            (patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2., patch.get_height()),
                            ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10)

plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • Customers with 'No Streaming Movies' have a higher churn rate of approximately 40.7%.
  • Customers with 'Yes Streaming Movies' have a slightly lower churn rate of about 25.9%.
  • Customers with 'No internet service' have the lowest churn rate at approximately 9.2%.

Similar to Streaming TV, customers without streaming movie services are somewhat more likely to churn.

15. Churn by Contract

In [62]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='Contract', hue='Churn', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Churn by Contract Type')
plt.xlabel('Contract Type')
plt.ylabel('Count')

# Pre-calculate churn percentages from the Data DataFrame
churn_percentages = Data.groupby('Contract')['Churn'].value_counts(normalize=True).unstack()

# Get the x-tick labels and their positions to map patch x-coordinates to category names
x_tick_positions = ax.get_xticks()
x_tick_labels = [label.get_text() for label in ax.get_xticklabels()]

# Assuming ax.containers[0] holds 'No Churn' bars and ax.containers[1] holds 'Churn' bars
if len(ax.containers) > 1:
    # Iterate over both containers (No Churn and Churn)
    for container_idx, container in enumerate(ax.containers):
        for patch in container:
            # Calculate the center x-coordinate of the bar
            bar_center_x = patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2

            # Find the corresponding category label based on the x-coordinate
            # This requires finding the closest x-tick position
            closest_tick_idx = (np.abs(x_tick_positions - bar_center_x)).argmin()
            current_category = x_tick_labels[closest_tick_idx]

            # Get the correct percentage based on container_idx (0 for No Churn, 1 for Churn)
            if current_category in churn_percentages.index:
                percentage_val = churn_percentages.loc[current_category, container_idx] * 100
                ax.annotate(f'{percentage_val:.1f}%',
                            (patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2., patch.get_height()),
                            ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10)

plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • Month-to-month contracts have the highest churn rate, with approximately 46.0%.
  • One year contracts show a much lower churn rate of about 11.3%.
  • Two year contracts have the lowest churn rate at approximately 3.3%.

This analysis highlights that contract duration is a critical factor influencing customer churn, with longer contracts leading to higher retention.

16. Churn by PaperlessBilling

In [63]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='PaperlessBilling', hue='Churn', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Churn by Paperless Billing')
plt.xlabel('Paperless Billing')
plt.ylabel('Count')

# Pre-calculate churn percentages from the Data DataFrame
churn_percentages = Data.groupby('PaperlessBilling')['Churn'].value_counts(normalize=True).unstack()

# Get the x-tick labels and their positions to map patch x-coordinates to category names
x_tick_positions = ax.get_xticks()
x_tick_labels = [label.get_text() for label in ax.get_xticklabels()]

# Assuming ax.containers[0] holds 'No Churn' bars and ax.containers[1] holds 'Churn' bars
if len(ax.containers) > 1:
    # Iterate over both containers (No Churn and Churn)
    for container_idx, container in enumerate(ax.containers):
        for patch in container:
            # Calculate the center x-coordinate of the bar
            bar_center_x = patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2

            # Find the corresponding category label based on the x-coordinate
            # This requires finding the closest x-tick position
            closest_tick_idx = (np.abs(x_tick_positions - bar_center_x)).argmin()
            current_category = x_tick_labels[closest_tick_idx]

            # Get the correct percentage based on container_idx (0 for No Churn, 1 for Churn)
            if current_category in churn_percentages.index:
                percentage_val = churn_percentages.loc[current_category, container_idx] * 100
                ax.annotate(f'{percentage_val:.1f}%',
                            (patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2., patch.get_height()),
                            ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10)

plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • Customers who opted for Paperless Billing (Yes) have a significantly higher churn rate of approximately 36.1%.
  • Customers who did not opt for Paperless Billing (No) have a much lower churn rate of approximately 20.0%.

This suggests that paperless billing might be correlated with higher churn, or it could be a characteristic of a customer segment more prone to churn.

17. Churn by PaymentMethod

In [64]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='PaymentMethod', hue='Churn', palette='viridis', order=Data['PaymentMethod'].value_counts().index)
plt.title('Churn by Payment Method')
plt.xlabel('Payment Method')
plt.ylabel('Count')

# Pre-calculate churn percentages from the Data DataFrame
churn_percentages = Data.groupby('PaymentMethod')['Churn'].value_counts(normalize=True).unstack()

# Get the x-tick labels and their positions to map patch x-coordinates to category names
x_tick_positions = ax.get_xticks()
x_tick_labels = [label.get_text() for label in ax.get_xticklabels()]

# Assuming ax.containers[0] holds 'No Churn' bars and ax.containers[1] holds 'Churn' bars
if len(ax.containers) > 1:
    # Iterate over both containers (No Churn and Churn)
    for container_idx, container in enumerate(ax.containers):
        for patch in container:
            # Calculate the center x-coordinate of the bar
            bar_center_x = patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2

            # Find the corresponding category label based on the x-coordinate
            # This requires finding the closest x-tick position
            closest_tick_idx = (np.abs(x_tick_positions - bar_center_x)).argmin()
            current_category = x_tick_labels[closest_tick_idx]

            # Get the correct percentage based on container_idx (0 for No Churn, 1 for Churn)
            if current_category in churn_percentages.index:
                percentage_val = churn_percentages.loc[current_category, container_idx] * 100
                ax.annotate(f'{percentage_val:.1f}%',
                            (patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2., patch.get_height()),
                            ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10)

plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha='right')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • Electronic check is associated with the highest churn rate, approximately 49.1%.
  • Mailed check has a churn rate of about 22.2%.
  • Bank transfer (automatic) has a churn rate of about 15.9%.
  • Credit card (automatic) has the lowest churn rate, approximately 14.1%.

This analysis suggests that manual payment methods, especially electronic checks, are strongly correlated with higher churn, while automatic payment methods like bank transfers and credit cards are associated with greater customer retention.

18. Churn by MonthlyCharges

In [65]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.histplot(data=Data, x='MonthlyCharges', hue='Churn', multiple='stack', palette='viridis', kde=True)
plt.title('Churn by Monthly Charges')
plt.xlabel('Monthly Charges ($)')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • The plot shows that customers with lower monthly charges have a higher propensity to churn. As monthly charges increase, the proportion of churned customers decreases, indicating that customers with higher spending are more loyal.

19. Churn by TotalCharges

In [66]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.histplot(data=Data, x='TotalCharges', hue='Churn', multiple='stack', palette='viridis', kde=True)
plt.title('Churn by Total Charges')
plt.xlabel('Total Charges ($)')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • The plot shows that customers with lower total charges have a higher propensity to churn. As total charges increase, the proportion of churned customers decreases, indicating that customers with higher cumulative spending are more loyal.

Multi-Variate Analysis¶

1. Heatmap of Numerical Features

In [67]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
sns.heatmap(Data.select_dtypes(include=np.number).corr(), annot=True, cmap='viridis', fmt='.2f', linewidths=.5)
plt.title('Correlation Heatmap of Numerical Features')
plt.show()

From the correlation heatmap, we can observe the following:

  • Tenure and TotalCharges: There is a strong positive correlation (0.63) between 'tenure' and 'TotalCharges'. This is expected, as customers with longer tenure are likely to accumulate higher total charges.

  • MonthlyCharges and TotalCharges: There is a strong positive correlation (0.55) between 'MonthlyCharges' and 'TotalCharges'. This also makes sense, as higher monthly charges contribute to higher total charges over time.

  • MonthlyCharges and Churn: There is a moderate positive correlation (0.19) between 'MonthlyCharges' and 'Churn'. This suggests that customers with higher monthly charges are slightly more likely to churn, which aligns with the bivariate analysis insights.

  • Tenure and Churn: There is a moderate negative correlation (-0.34) between 'tenure' and 'Churn'. This indicates that customers with longer tenure are less likely to churn.

  • SeniorCitizen and Churn: There is a weak positive correlation (0.12) between 'SeniorCitizen' and 'Churn'. This suggests that senior citizens are slightly more likely to churn, consistent with the earlier bivariate analysis.

Other correlations among numerical features are relatively weak, indicating that these variables largely have independent effects on churn or other numerical variables.

2. Pair Plot of Numerical Features

In [68]:
numerical_cols_for_pairplot = Data.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns.tolist()
# Remove 'Churn' from the list of numerical columns that will be plotted as x/y variables
if 'Churn' in numerical_cols_for_pairplot:
    numerical_cols_for_pairplot.remove('Churn')

# Now, create the pairplot. The `vars` argument specifies which columns to use for the grid axes.
# The `hue` argument specifies the column to color by.
sns.pairplot(Data, vars=numerical_cols_for_pairplot, hue='Churn', palette='viridis', diag_kind='kde')
plt.suptitle('Pair Plot of Numerical Features by Churn Status', y=1.02)
plt.show()

This pair plot provides a comprehensive view of the relationships between all numerical features ('SeniorCitizen', 'tenure', 'MonthlyCharges', 'TotalCharges') and their distributions, with the 'Churn' status highlighted. Key observations include:

  • Diagonal plots: Show the distribution of each numerical feature for churned and non-churned customers. For instance, 'tenure' and 'TotalCharges' clearly show that non-churned customers tend to have higher values, while 'MonthlyCharges' indicates a more complex relationship.

Outlier Detection using Box Plots¶

1. Box Plot for SeniorCitizen

In [69]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
sns.boxplot(y=Data['SeniorCitizen'], palette='viridis')
plt.title('Box Plot of SeniorCitizen')
plt.ylabel('SeniorCitizen (0: No, 1: Yes)')
plt.show()

This box plot for 'SeniorCitizen' confirms its binary nature (0 or 1). There are no outliers in the traditional sense, as all values fall within the expected range for a binary variable.

2. Box Plot for Tenure

In [70]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
sns.boxplot(y=Data['tenure'], palette='viridis')
plt.title('Box Plot of Tenure')
plt.ylabel('Tenure (Months)')
plt.show()

The box plot for 'tenure' shows the distribution of customer tenure in months. While there might be some data points at the very low end, these represent new customers and are not considered erroneous outliers.

3. Box Plot for MonthlyCharges

In [71]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
sns.boxplot(y=Data['MonthlyCharges'], palette='viridis')
plt.title('Box Plot of MonthlyCharges')
plt.ylabel('Monthly Charges ($)')
plt.show()

The box plot for 'MonthlyCharges' shows a relatively spread-out distribution. But there are not any potential outliers.

4. Box Plot for TotalCharges

In [72]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
sns.boxplot(y=Data['TotalCharges'], palette='viridis')
plt.title('Box Plot of TotalCharges')
plt.ylabel('Total Charges ($)')
plt.show()

The box plot for 'TotalCharges' displays a right-skewed distribution with a notable number of data points extending far above the upper whisker. These indicate customers with very high cumulative charges, which is expected for long-term customers with high monthly bills. These are considered valid data points and not outliers to be removed, as they provide valuable information about high-value customers.

5. Box Plot for Churn

In [73]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
sns.boxplot(y=Data['Churn'], palette='viridis')
plt.title('Box Plot of Churn')
plt.ylabel('Churn (0: No, 1: Yes)')
plt.show()

The box plot for 'Churn' simply reflects its binary nature (0 or 1).

Exploring New Features¶

1. TenureGroup¶

The univariate analysis of 'tenure' showed bimaodal distribution. Creating a categorical 'tenure_group' can provide a clearer understanding of how customer loyalty influences churn. We will categorize 'tenure' into bins such as '0-12 Months', '13-24 Months', etc.

In [74]:
# Define bins for tenure groups
bins = [-1, 12, 24, 36, 48, 60, Data['tenure'].max()]
labels = ['0-12 Months', '13-24 Months', '25-36 Months', '37-48 Months', '49-60 Months', '> 60 Months']

# Create the new 'TenureGroup' feature
Data['TenureGroup'] = pd.cut(Data['tenure'], bins=bins, labels=labels, right=True)

print("New feature 'TenureGroup' created successfully.")
display(Data[['tenure', 'TenureGroup']].head())

New feature 'TenureGroup' created successfully.
tenure TenureGroup
0 1.0 0-12 Months
1 34.0 25-36 Months
2 2.0 0-12 Months
3 45.0 37-48 Months
4 2.0 0-12 Months

1. Distribution of 'TenureGroup'¶

In [75]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='TenureGroup', palette='viridis', order=labels)
plt.title('Distribution of Tenure Groups')
plt.xlabel('Tenure Group')
plt.ylabel('Count')

total = len(Data['TenureGroup'])
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * p.get_height()/total)
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha='right')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
  • The distribution of 'TenureGroup' shows that a large proportion of customers are in the '0-12 Months' group, indicating a significant number of new customers. There's also a substantial portion in the '> 60 Months' group, suggesting a loyal customer base. The middle tenure groups have fewer customers.

2. Churn by 'TenureGroup'¶

In [76]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='TenureGroup', hue='Churn', palette='viridis', order=labels)
plt.title('Churn by Tenure Group')
plt.xlabel('Tenure Group')
plt.ylabel('Count')

# Pre-calculate churn percentages from the Data DataFrame
churn_percentages = Data.groupby('TenureGroup')['Churn'].value_counts(normalize=True).unstack()

# Get the x-tick labels and their positions to map patch x-coordinates to category names
x_tick_positions = ax.get_xticks()
x_tick_labels = [label.get_text() for label in ax.get_xticklabels()]

# Assuming ax.containers[0] holds 'No Churn' bars and ax.containers[1] holds 'Churn' bars
if len(ax.containers) > 1:
    # Iterate over both containers (No Churn and Churn)
    for container_idx, container in enumerate(ax.containers):
        for patch in container:
            # Calculate the center x-coordinate of the bar
            bar_center_x = patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2

            # Find the corresponding category label based on the x-coordinate
            # This requires finding the closest x-tick position
            closest_tick_idx = (np.abs(x_tick_positions - bar_center_x)).argmin()
            current_category = x_tick_labels[closest_tick_idx]

            # Get the correct percentage based on container_idx (0 for No Churn, 1 for Churn)
            if current_category in churn_percentages.index:
                percentage_val = churn_percentages.loc[current_category, container_idx] * 100
                ax.annotate(f'{percentage_val:.1f}%',
                            (patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2., patch.get_height()),
                            ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10)

plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha='right')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • '0-12 Months' Group: This group has the highest churn rate, significantly higher than all other tenure groups.
  • Higher Tenure Groups: The churn rate generally decreases as tenure increases. Customers in the '> 60 Months' group have the lowest churn rate, indicating strong loyalty.

2. HasInternet¶

This feature will be a binary indicator, '1' if the customer has any internet service (DSL or Fiber optic) and '0' if they do not.

In [77]:
Data['HasInternet'] = Data['InternetService'].apply(lambda x: 1 if x != 'No' else 0)
print("New feature 'HasInternet' created successfully.")
display(Data[['InternetService', 'HasInternet']].head())

New feature 'HasInternet' created successfully.
InternetService HasInternet
0 DSL 1
1 DSL 1
2 DSL 1
3 DSL 1
4 Fiber optic 1

1. Distribution of 'HasInternet'¶

In [78]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='HasInternet', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Distribution of Has Internet Service')
plt.xlabel('Has Internet (0: No, 1: Yes)')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.xticks(ticks=[0, 1], labels=['No Internet', 'Has Internet'])

total = len(Data['HasInternet'])
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * p.get_height()/total)
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.show()
  • The majority of customers (76.6%) have internet service, while 23.4% do not.

2. Churn by 'HasInternet'¶

In [79]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='HasInternet', hue='Churn', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Churn by Has Internet Service')
plt.xlabel('Has Internet (0: No, 1: Yes)')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.xticks(ticks=[0, 1], labels=['No Internet', 'Has Internet'])

# Pre-calculate churn percentages from the Data DataFrame
churn_percentages = Data.groupby('HasInternet')['Churn'].value_counts(normalize=True).unstack()

# Get the x-tick labels and their positions to map patch x-coordinates to category names
x_tick_positions = ax.get_xticks()
x_tick_labels = [label.get_text() for label in ax.get_xticklabels()]

# Assuming ax.containers[0] holds 'No Churn' bars and ax.containers[1] holds 'Churn' bars
if len(ax.containers) > 1:
    # Iterate over both containers (No Churn and Churn)
    for container_idx, container in enumerate(ax.containers):
        for patch in container:
            # Calculate the center x-coordinate of the bar
            bar_center_x = patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2

            # Find the corresponding category label based on the x-coordinate
            # This requires finding the closest x-tick position
            closest_tick_idx = (np.abs(x_tick_positions - bar_center_x)).argmin()
            current_category_label = x_tick_labels[closest_tick_idx]

            # Convert current_category_label string label back to numerical key for indexing churn_percentages
            if current_category_label == 'No Internet':
                category_key = 0
            elif current_category_label == 'Has Internet':
                category_key = 1
            else:
                category_key = None # Fallback, should not happen with only two categories

            if category_key is not None and category_key in churn_percentages.index:
                percentage_val = churn_percentages.loc[category_key, container_idx] * 100
                ax.annotate(f'{percentage_val:.1f}%',
                            (patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2., patch.get_height()),
                            ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10)

plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • Customers who do not have internet service ('No Internet') have a very low churn rate of approximately 9.5%.
  • Customers who do have internet service ('Has Internet') have a significantly higher churn rate of approximately 34.0%.

This confirms that having internet service is strongly associated with a higher likelihood of churn, likely due to the specific challenges or dissatisfaction with internet services (Like with Fiber Optic having high churn).

3. HasSecurity¶

This feature will indicate whether a customer subscribes to any of the internet security related services like OnlineSecurity, OnlineBackup, DeviceProtection, or TechSupport.

In [80]:
security_features = ['OnlineSecurity', 'OnlineBackup', 'DeviceProtection', 'TechSupport']
Data['HasSecurity'] = Data[security_features].apply(lambda x: 1 if any(s == 'Yes' for s in x) else 0, axis=1)
print("New feature 'HasSecurity' created successfully.")
display(Data[security_features + ['HasSecurity']].head())

New feature 'HasSecurity' created successfully.
OnlineSecurity OnlineBackup DeviceProtection TechSupport HasSecurity
0 No Yes No No 1
1 Yes No Yes No 1
2 Yes Yes No No 1
3 Yes No Yes Yes 1
4 No No No No 0

1. Distribution of 'HasSecurity'¶

In [81]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='HasSecurity', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Distribution of Has Security Features')
plt.xlabel('Has Security Features (0: No, 1: Yes)')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.xticks(ticks=[0, 1], labels=['No Security Features', 'Has Security Features'])

total = len(Data['HasSecurity'])
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * p.get_height()/total)
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.show()
  • Approximately 41.80% of customers do not have any of these security-related features, while 58.2% have at least one.

2. Churn by 'HasSecurity'¶

In [82]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='HasSecurity', hue='Churn', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Churn by Has Security Features')
plt.xlabel('Has Security Features (0: No, 1: Yes)')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.xticks(ticks=[0, 1], labels=['No Security Features', 'Has Security Features'])

# Pre-calculate churn percentages from the Data DataFrame
churn_percentages = Data.groupby('HasSecurity')['Churn'].value_counts(normalize=True).unstack()

# Get the x-tick labels and their positions to map patch x-coordinates to category names
x_tick_positions = ax.get_xticks()
x_tick_labels = [label.get_text() for label in ax.get_xticklabels()]

# Assuming ax.containers[0] holds 'No Churn' bars and ax.containers[1] holds 'Churn' bars
if len(ax.containers) > 1:
    # Iterate over both containers (No Churn and Churn)
    for container_idx, container in enumerate(ax.containers):
        for patch in container:
            # Calculate the center x-coordinate of the bar
            bar_center_x = patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2

            # Find the corresponding category label based on the x-coordinate
            # This requires finding the closest x-tick position
            closest_tick_idx = (np.abs(x_tick_positions - bar_center_x)).argmin()
            current_category_label = x_tick_labels[closest_tick_idx]

            # Convert current_category_label string label back to numerical key for indexing churn_percentages
            if current_category_label == 'No Security Features':
                category_key = 0
            elif current_category_label == 'Has Security Features':
                category_key = 1
            else:
                category_key = None # Fallback, should not happen with only two categories

            if category_key is not None and category_key in churn_percentages.index:
                percentage_val = churn_percentages.loc[category_key, container_idx] * 100
                ax.annotate(f'{percentage_val:.1f}%',
                            (patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2., patch.get_height()),
                            ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10)

plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • Customers who do not have any security features have a significantly higher churn rate of approximately 31.9%.
  • Customers who have at least one security feature have lower churn rate of approximately 25.6%.

This indicates that providing security related services is a strong factor in customer retention. Customers opting for these features are far less likely to churn.

4.TotalServices¶

This feature will represent the total number of services (phone, multiple lines, Internet, online security, online backup, device protection, tech support, streaming TV, streaming movies) which a customer subscribes.

In [83]:
service_columns = [
    'PhoneService',
    'MultipleLines',
    'InternetService',
    'OnlineSecurity',
    'OnlineBackup',
    'DeviceProtection',
    'TechSupport',
    'StreamingTV',
    'StreamingMovies'
]

# Helper function to count 'Yes' or 'No' services
def count_active_services(row):
    count = 0
    if row['PhoneService'] == 'Yes':
        count += 1
    if row['MultipleLines'] == 'Yes':
        count += 1
    if row['InternetService'] != 'No': # DSL or Fiber optic
        count += 1
    # For the following, 'No internet service' implies 'No', so 'Yes' is the only positive indicator
    if row['OnlineSecurity'] == 'Yes':
        count += 1
    if row['OnlineBackup'] == 'Yes':
        count += 1
    if row['DeviceProtection'] == 'Yes':
        count += 1
    if row['TechSupport'] == 'Yes':
        count += 1
    if row['StreamingTV'] == 'Yes':
        count += 1
    if row['StreamingMovies'] == 'Yes':
        count += 1
    return count

Data['TotalServices'] = Data.apply(count_active_services, axis=1)
print("New feature 'TotalServices' created successfully.")
display(Data[service_columns + ['TotalServices']].head())

New feature 'TotalServices' created successfully.
PhoneService MultipleLines InternetService OnlineSecurity OnlineBackup DeviceProtection TechSupport StreamingTV StreamingMovies TotalServices
0 No No phone service DSL No Yes No No No No 2
1 Yes No DSL Yes No Yes No No No 4
2 Yes No DSL Yes Yes No No No No 4
3 No No phone service DSL Yes No Yes Yes No No 4
4 Yes No Fiber optic No No No No No No 2

1. Distribution of 'TotalServices'¶

In [84]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='TotalServices', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Distribution of Total Services')
plt.xlabel('Number of Services')
plt.ylabel('Count')

total = len(Data['TotalServices'])
for p in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(100 * p.get_height()/total)
    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2
    y = p.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x, y), ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.show()
  • The distribution shows that many customers have a low number of services (around 1-3), while there's also a considerable number of customers with a higher number of services (around 6-7). This indicates a diverse customer base in terms of service adoption.

2. Churn by 'TotalServices'¶

In [85]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
ax = sns.countplot(data=Data, x='TotalServices', hue='Churn', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Churn by Total Services')
plt.xlabel('Number of Services')
plt.ylabel('Count')

# Pre-calculate churn percentages from the Data DataFrame
churn_percentages = Data.groupby('TotalServices')['Churn'].value_counts(normalize=True).unstack()

# Get the x-tick labels and their positions to map patch x-coordinates to category names
x_tick_positions = ax.get_xticks()
x_tick_labels = [label.get_text() for label in ax.get_xticklabels()]

# Assuming ax.containers[0] holds 'No Churn' bars and ax.containers[1] holds 'Churn' bars
if len(ax.containers) > 1:
    # Iterate over both containers (No Churn and Churn)
    for container_idx, container in enumerate(ax.containers):
        for patch in container:
            # Calculate the center x-coordinate of the bar
            bar_center_x = patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2

            # Find the corresponding category label based on the x-coordinate
            # This requires finding the closest x-tick position
            closest_tick_idx = (np.abs(x_tick_positions - bar_center_x)).argmin()
            current_category_label = x_tick_labels[closest_tick_idx]

            # Convert current_category_label string label back to numerical key for indexing churn_percentages
            category_key = int(current_category_label)

            if category_key in churn_percentages.index:
                percentage_val = churn_percentages.loc[category_key, container_idx] * 100
                ax.annotate(f'{percentage_val:.1f}%',
                            (patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2., patch.get_height()),
                            ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10)

plt.legend(title='Churn', labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
plt.show()
  • The churn rate appears to be highest for customers with a moderate number of services (around 2-3 services).
  • Customers with only one service have a relatively low churn rate, possibly aligning with those who have 'No internet service'.
  • Customers with a high number of services (6-9) also show a lower churn rate, suggesting that customers who are more deeply embedded with the company's offerings are more loyal.

This feature provides a nuanced view, suggesting that either very basic or very comprehensive service bundles might lead to higher retention, while a middle-ground offering might be more prone to churn.

Evaluation Metrics¶

The target variable is binary (churn or no churn) and the dataset is imbalanced (fewer churned customers than non-churned), specific evaluation metrics are more appropriate than simple accuracy.

Here are the chosen metrics with their rationale:

  1. Recall for the 'Churn' class :

    • Rationale: Recall measures the proportion of actual churners that were correctly identified by the model. (minimize False Negatives) Missing a churner means losing a customer, which directly impacts revenue.

  2. Precision for the 'Churn' class:

    • Rationale: Precision measures the proportion of customers predicted as churners who actually churned.(minimize False Positives). High precision ensures that retention efforts are targeted effectively and resources are not wasted on customers who wouldn't churn anyway.

  3. F1-Score:

    • Rationale: The F1-Score is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. A high F1-score indicates a good balance between identifying positive cases and not making too many false positive predictions.

  4. ROC AUC (Receiver Operating Characteristic - Area Under the Curve):

    • Rationale: ROC AUC measures the ability of the model to distinguish between positive and negative classes across various classification thresholds. A higher AUC indicates a better model performance in separating churners from non-churners.

In summary, while we will monitor accuracy, the primary focus will be on Recall, Precision, and F1-Score for the 'Churn' class, and ROC AUC for overall model performance, to ensure that the model is effective in identifying and acting upon potential customer churn.

Building a Baseline Model (Logistic Regression)¶

We will build a Logistic Regression model as our first baseline model.

Data Preprocessing¶

In [86]:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# Separate features (X) and target (y)
X = Data.drop('Churn', axis=1)
y = Data['Churn']

# Identify categorical and numerical columns
categorical_cols = X.select_dtypes(include=['object', 'category']).columns
numerical_cols = X.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns

# One-hot encode categorical features
X_encoded = pd.get_dummies(X, columns=categorical_cols, drop_first=True)

# Split the data into training+validation (85%) and testing (15%) sets
X_train_val, X_test, y_train_val, y_test = train_test_split(X_encoded, y, test_size=0.15, random_state=42, stratify=y)

# Split the training+validation data into training (70% of total) and validation (15% of total) sets
# test_size here is 0.15 / (1 - 0.15) = 0.15 / 0.85.
X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(X_train_val, y_train_val, test_size=(0.15/(0.85)), random_state=42, stratify=y_train_val)

# Scale numerical features using StandardScaler
# Fit on training data only to prevent data leakage
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_train[numerical_cols] = scaler.fit_transform(X_train[numerical_cols])
X_val[numerical_cols] = scaler.transform(X_val[numerical_cols])
X_test[numerical_cols] = scaler.transform(X_test[numerical_cols])

print("Data preprocessing complete. Data split into training, validation, and testing sets, and features are scaled.")
print(f"X_train shape: {X_train.shape}")
print(f"X_val shape: {X_val.shape}")
print(f"X_test shape: {X_test.shape}")
print(f"y_train shape: {y_train.shape}")
print(f"y_val shape: {y_val.shape}")
print(f"y_test shape: {y_test.shape}")

Data preprocessing complete. Data split into training, validation, and testing sets, and features are scaled.
X_train shape: (8435, 38)
X_val shape: (1808, 38)
X_test shape: (1808, 38)
y_train shape: (8435,)
y_val shape: (1808,)
y_test shape: (1808,)

Training the Logistic Regression Model¶

In [87]:
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

# Initialize and train the Logistic Regression model
log_reg = LogisticRegression(random_state=42, solver='liblinear')
log_reg.fit(X_train, y_train)

print("Logistic Regression model trained successfully.")

Logistic Regression model trained successfully.
In [88]:
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, roc_auc_score, confusion_matrix, ConfusionMatrixDisplay
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Make predictions on the validation set
y_pred_val_lr = log_reg.predict(X_val)
y_pred_proba_val_lr = log_reg.predict_proba(X_val)[:, 1]

# Confusion Matrix
print("\nConfusion Matrix (Logistic Regression - Validation Set):")
cm_val_lr = confusion_matrix(y_val, y_pred_val_lr)
cmd_val_lr = ConfusionMatrixDisplay(confusion_matrix=cm_val_lr, display_labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
cmd_val_lr.plot(cmap='viridis')
plt.title('Confusion Matrix (Logistic Regression - Validation Set)')
plt.show()

# Classification Report (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score)
print("\nClassification Report (Logistic Regression - Validation Set):")
print(classification_report(y_val, y_pred_val_lr, target_names=['No Churn', 'Churn']))

# ROC AUC Score
roc_auc_val_lr = roc_auc_score(y_val, y_pred_proba_val_lr)
print(f"\nROC AUC Score (Logistic Regression - Validation Set): {roc_auc_val_lr:.4f}")

Confusion Matrix (Logistic Regression - Validation Set):

Classification Report (Logistic Regression - Validation Set):
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

    No Churn       0.86      0.91      0.88      1298
       Churn       0.72      0.61      0.66       510

    accuracy                           0.82      1808
   macro avg       0.79      0.76      0.77      1808
weighted avg       0.82      0.82      0.82      1808


ROC AUC Score (Logistic Regression - Validation Set): 0.8638

Decision Tree Classifier¶

We will build Decision Tree model as our second baseline model.

In [89]:
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier

# Initialize and train the Decision Tree Classifier model
dtc = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=42)
dtc.fit(X_train, y_train)

print("Decision Tree Classifier model trained successfully.")

Decision Tree Classifier model trained successfully.

Model Performance Evaluation (Decision Tree Classifier)¶

In [90]:
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, roc_auc_score, confusion_matrix, ConfusionMatrixDisplay

# Make predictions on the validation set
y_pred_dt = dtc.predict(X_val)
y_pred_proba_dt = dtc.predict_proba(X_val)[:, 1]

# Confusion Matrix
print("\nConfusion Matrix (Decision Tree - Validation Set):")
cm_dt = confusion_matrix(y_val, y_pred_dt)
cmd_dt = ConfusionMatrixDisplay(confusion_matrix=cm_dt, display_labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
cmd_dt.plot(cmap='viridis')
plt.title('Confusion Matrix (Decision Tree - Validation Set)')
plt.show()

# Classification Report (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score)
print("\nClassification Report (Decision Tree - Validation Set):")
print(classification_report(y_val, y_pred_dt, target_names=['No Churn', 'Churn']))

# ROC AUC Score
roc_auc_dt = roc_auc_score(y_val, y_pred_proba_dt)
print(f"\nROC AUC Score (Decision Tree - Validation Set): {roc_auc_dt:.4f}")

Confusion Matrix (Decision Tree - Validation Set):

Classification Report (Decision Tree - Validation Set):
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

    No Churn       0.83      0.80      0.81      1298
       Churn       0.52      0.57      0.55       510

    accuracy                           0.73      1808
   macro avg       0.67      0.68      0.68      1808
weighted avg       0.74      0.73      0.74      1808


ROC AUC Score (Decision Tree - Validation Set): 0.6842

K-Nearest Neighbors Classifier¶

We will build K-Nearest Neighbors Classifier model as our third baseline model.

In [91]:
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier

# Initialize and train the KNN Classifier model
knn = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=5)
knn.fit(X_train, y_train)

print("K-Nearest Neighbors Classifier model trained successfully.")

K-Nearest Neighbors Classifier model trained successfully.

# ROC AUC Score
roc_auc_knn = roc_auc_score(y_val, y_pred_proba_knn)
print(f"\nROC AUC Score (K-Nearest Neighbors - Validation Set): {roc_auc_knn:.4f}")

XGBoost Classifier¶

We will build an XGBoost Classifier model.

In [101]:
from xgboost import XGBClassifier

# Initialize and train the XGBoost Classifier model
xgb_clf = XGBClassifier(random_state=42, use_label_encoder=False, eval_metric='logloss')
xgb_clf.fit(X_train, y_train)

print("XGBoost Classifier model trained successfully.")

XGBoost Classifier model trained successfully.

Model Performance Evaluation (XGBoost Classifier)¶

In [102]:
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, roc_auc_score, confusion_matrix, ConfusionMatrixDisplay
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Make predictions on the validation set
y_pred_xgb = xgb_clf.predict(X_val)
y_pred_proba_xgb = xgb_clf.predict_proba(X_val)[:, 1]

# Confusion Matrix
print("\nConfusion Matrix (XGBoost Classifier - Validation Set):")
cm_xgb = confusion_matrix(y_val, y_pred_xgb)
cmd_xgb = ConfusionMatrixDisplay(confusion_matrix=cm_xgb, display_labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
cmd_xgb.plot(cmap='viridis')
plt.title('Confusion Matrix (XGBoost Classifier - Validation Set)')
plt.show()

# Classification Report (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score)
print("\nClassification Report (XGBoost Classifier - Validation Set):")
print(classification_report(y_val, y_pred_xgb, target_names=['No Churn', 'Churn']))

# ROC AUC Score
roc_auc_xgb = roc_auc_score(y_val, y_pred_proba_xgb)
print(f"\nROC AUC Score (XGBoost Classifier - Validation Set): {roc_auc_xgb:.4f}")

Confusion Matrix (XGBoost Classifier - Validation Set):

Classification Report (XGBoost Classifier - Validation Set):
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

    No Churn       0.84      0.89      0.87      1298
       Churn       0.68      0.58      0.63       510

    accuracy                           0.80      1808
   macro avg       0.76      0.74      0.75      1808
weighted avg       0.80      0.80      0.80      1808


ROC AUC Score (XGBoost Classifier - Validation Set): 0.8509

Model Performance Comparison¶

Let's compare the performance of all the models on the validation set using our chosen evaluation metrics.

In [103]:
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score, roc_auc_score

# Dictionary to store results for each model
results = {
    'Model': [],
    'Accuracy': [],
    'Precision (Churn)': [],
    'Recall (Churn)': [],
    'F1-Score (Churn)': [],
    'ROC AUC': []
}

def add_model_results(model_name, y_true, y_pred, y_proba):
    results['Model'].append(model_name)
    results['Accuracy'].append(accuracy_score(y_true, y_pred))
    results['Precision (Churn)'].append(precision_score(y_true, y_pred, pos_label=1))
    results['Recall (Churn)'].append(recall_score(y_true, y_pred, pos_label=1))
    results['F1-Score (Churn)'].append(f1_score(y_true, y_pred, pos_label=1))
    results['ROC AUC'].append(roc_auc_score(y_true, y_proba))

# Add results for Logistic Regression
add_model_results('Logistic Regression', y_val, y_pred_val_lr, y_pred_proba_val_lr)

# Add results for Decision Tree
add_model_results('Decision Tree', y_val, y_pred_dt, y_pred_proba_dt)

# Add results for K-Nearest Neighbors
add_model_results('K-Nearest Neighbors', y_val, y_pred_knn, y_pred_proba_knn)

# Add results for Bagging Classifier
add_model_results('Bagging Classifier', y_val, y_pred_bagging, y_pred_proba_bagging)

# Add results for Random Forest Classifier
add_model_results('Random Forest Classifier', y_val, y_pred_rf, y_pred_proba_rf)

# Add results for AdaBoost Classifier
add_model_results('AdaBoost Classifier', y_val, y_pred_adaboost, y_pred_proba_adaboost)

# Add results for Gradient Boosting Classifier
add_model_results('Gradient Boosting Classifier', y_val, y_pred_gbc, y_pred_proba_gbc)

# Add results for XGBoost Classifier
add_model_results('XGBoost Classifier', y_val, y_pred_xgb, y_pred_proba_xgb)

# Create DataFrame
results_df = pd.DataFrame(results)
results_df = results_df.round(4)

# Display the results table
display(results_df.sort_values(by='ROC AUC', ascending=False))
Model Accuracy Precision (Churn) Recall (Churn) F1-Score (Churn) ROC AUC
6 Gradient Boosting Classifier 0.8213 0.7210 0.5980 0.6538 0.8647
0 Logistic Regression 0.8236 0.7195 0.6137 0.6624 0.8638
5 AdaBoost Classifier 0.8059 0.6845 0.5784 0.6270 0.8569
7 XGBoost Classifier 0.8042 0.6789 0.5804 0.6258 0.8509
4 Random Forest Classifier 0.8103 0.6956 0.5824 0.6339 0.8479
3 Bagging Classifier 0.7942 0.6708 0.5314 0.5930 0.8232
2 K-Nearest Neighbors 0.7572 0.5697 0.5686 0.5692 0.7875
1 Decision Tree 0.7329 0.5242 0.5725 0.5473 0.6842

Observations¶

Based on the evaluation metrics, particularly focusing on Recall (Churn), Precision (Churn), F1-Score (Churn), and ROC AUC, we can make the following observations:

  • Overall Best Performers:

    • Logistic Regression and Gradient Boosting Classifier show the best overall performance with the highest ROC AUC scores (0.8638 and 0.8647 respectively). These models also exhibit a good balance across precision and recall for the churn class.

  • Recall (Churn):

    • Logistic Regression (0.61) and Gradient Boosting Classifier (0.60), AdaBoost Classifier (0.58) and XGBoost Classifier (0.58), and Random Forest Classifier (0.58) achieve relatively good recall scores, meaning they are better at identifying actual churners. This is crucial for AlphaCom to proactively target at-risk customers.

  • Precision (Churn):

    • Logistic Regression (0.72) and Gradient Boosting Classifier (0.72) demonstrate the highest precision for the churn class. This means that when these models predict a customer will churn, they are more likely to be correct, leading to more efficient resource allocation for retention campaigns.
    • AdaBoost Classifier (0.68), XGBoost Classifier (0.68), Bagging Classifier (0.67), and Random Forest Classifier (0.70) also show decent precision scores.
    • Decision Tree (0.52) and K-Nearest Neighbors (0.57) have the lowest precision, indicating a higher rate of false positives, which could lead to wasted retention efforts.

  • F1-Score (Churn):

    • Logistic Regression (0.66) and Gradient Boosting Classifier (0.65) lead in F1-score, suggesting a strong balance between identifying churners and doing so accurately. This is a robust indicator for overall effectiveness in churn prediction.

  • ROC AUC:

    • Gradient Boosting Classifier (0.8647) and Logistic Regression (0.8638) have the highest ROC AUC scores, indicating their superior ability to distinguish between churners and non-churners across various classification thresholds. This makes them highly reliable for ranking customers by their churn risk.

From these obervations we will choose Gradient Boosting Classifier, Logistic Regression, AdaBoost Classifier, XGBoost Classifier and Random Forest Classifier for tunning.

Hyperparameter Tuning for Gradient Boosting Classifier¶

In [104]:
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

# Define the parameter grid to search
param_grid = {
    'n_estimators': [50, 100, 200], # Number of boosting stages
    'learning_rate': [0.01, 0.1, 0.2], # Step size shrinkage
    'max_depth': [3, 5, 7] # Maximum depth of the individual regression estimators
}

# Initialize Gradient Boosting Classifier
gbc = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=42)

# Initialize GridSearchCV
grid_search_gbc = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=gbc,
    param_grid=param_grid,
    scoring='roc_auc', # Optimize for ROC AUC, as identified earlier
    cv=3, # Use 3-fold cross-validation
    n_jobs=-1, # Use all available cores
    verbose=2 # Display progress
)

# Fit GridSearchCV to the training data
grid_search_gbc.fit(X_train, y_train)

print("GridSearchCV for Gradient Boosting Classifier completed successfully.")
print(f"Best parameters found: {grid_search_gbc.best_params_}")
print(f"Best ROC AUC score: {grid_search_gbc.best_score_:.4f}")

Fitting 3 folds for each of 27 candidates, totalling 81 fits
GridSearchCV for Gradient Boosting Classifier completed successfully.
Best parameters found: {'learning_rate': 0.1, 'max_depth': 3, 'n_estimators': 50}
Best ROC AUC score: 0.8469

Evaluate Tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier¶

In [105]:
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, roc_auc_score, confusion_matrix, ConfusionMatrixDisplay
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Get the best model from GridSearchCV
best_gbc = grid_search_gbc.best_estimator_

# Make predictions on the validation set using the best model
y_pred_gbc_tuned = best_gbc.predict(X_val)
y_pred_proba_gbc_tuned = best_gbc.predict_proba(X_val)[:, 1]

# Confusion Matrix
print("\nConfusion Matrix (Tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier - Validation Set):")
cm_gbc_tuned = confusion_matrix(y_val, y_pred_gbc_tuned)
cmd_gbc_tuned = ConfusionMatrixDisplay(confusion_matrix=cm_gbc_tuned, display_labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
cmd_gbc_tuned.plot(cmap='viridis')
plt.title('Confusion Matrix (Tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier - Validation Set)')
plt.show()

# Classification Report (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score)
print("\nClassification Report (Tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier - Validation Set):")
print(classification_report(y_val, y_pred_gbc_tuned, target_names=['No Churn', 'Churn']))

# ROC AUC Score
roc_auc_gbc_tuned = roc_auc_score(y_val, y_pred_proba_gbc_tuned)
print(f"\nROC AUC Score (Tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier - Validation Set): {roc_auc_gbc_tuned:.4f}")

Confusion Matrix (Tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier - Validation Set):

Classification Report (Tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier - Validation Set):
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

    No Churn       0.85      0.92      0.88      1298
       Churn       0.73      0.59      0.65       510

    accuracy                           0.82      1808
   macro avg       0.79      0.75      0.77      1808
weighted avg       0.82      0.82      0.82      1808


ROC AUC Score (Tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier - Validation Set): 0.8634

Hyperparameter Tuning for Logistic Regression¶

In [106]:
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

# Define the parameter grid to search
param_grid_lr = {
    'penalty': ['l1', 'l2'], # Regularization penalty
    'C': [0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100], # Inverse of regularization strength
    'solver': ['liblinear'] # Solver that supports both l1 and l2 penalties
}

# Initialize Logistic Regression model
log_reg_tune = LogisticRegression(random_state=42)

# Initialize GridSearchCV
grid_search_lr = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=log_reg_tune,
    param_grid=param_grid_lr,
    scoring='roc_auc', # Optimize for ROC AUC
    cv=3, # Use 3-fold cross-validation
    n_jobs=-1, # Use all available cores
    verbose=2 # Display progress
)

# Fit GridSearchCV to the training data
grid_search_lr.fit(X_train, y_train)

print("GridSearchCV for Logistic Regression completed successfully.")
print(f"Best parameters found: {grid_search_lr.best_params_}")
print(f"Best ROC AUC score: {grid_search_lr.best_score_:.4f}")

Fitting 3 folds for each of 12 candidates, totalling 36 fits
GridSearchCV for Logistic Regression completed successfully.
Best parameters found: {'C': 0.1, 'penalty': 'l1', 'solver': 'liblinear'}
Best ROC AUC score: 0.8472

Evaluate Tuned Logistic Regression Classifier¶

In [107]:
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, roc_auc_score, confusion_matrix, ConfusionMatrixDisplay
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Get the best model from GridSearchCV
best_log_reg = grid_search_lr.best_estimator_

# Make predictions on the validation set using the best model
y_pred_lr_tuned = best_log_reg.predict(X_val)
y_pred_proba_lr_tuned = best_log_reg.predict_proba(X_val)[:, 1]

# Confusion Matrix
print("\nConfusion Matrix (Tuned Logistic Regression - Validation Set):")
cm_lr_tuned = confusion_matrix(y_val, y_pred_lr_tuned)
cmd_lr_tuned = ConfusionMatrixDisplay(confusion_matrix=cm_lr_tuned, display_labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
cmd_lr_tuned.plot(cmap='viridis')
plt.title('Confusion Matrix (Tuned Logistic Regression - Validation Set)')
plt.show()

# Classification Report (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score)
print("\nClassification Report (Tuned Logistic Regression - Validation Set):")
print(classification_report(y_val, y_pred_lr_tuned, target_names=['No Churn', 'Churn']))

# ROC AUC Score
roc_auc_lr_tuned = roc_auc_score(y_val, y_pred_proba_lr_tuned)
print(f"\nROC AUC Score (Tuned Logistic Regression - Validation Set): {roc_auc_lr_tuned:.4f}")

Hyperparameter Tuning for AdaBoost Classifier¶

In [108]:
from sklearn.ensemble import AdaBoostClassifier
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

# Define the base estimator for AdaBoost. DecisionTree is commonly used.
base_estimator = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=42, max_depth=1) # Limiting max_depth for faster tuning and to prevent overfitting of base estimators

# Define the parameter grid to search
param_grid_adaboost = {
    'n_estimators': [50, 100, 200], # Number of boosting rounds
    'learning_rate': [0.01, 0.1, 1.0] # Step size shrinkage
}

# Initialize AdaBoost Classifier with the base estimator
adaboost_tune = AdaBoostClassifier(estimator=base_estimator, random_state=42)

# Initialize GridSearchCV
grid_search_adaboost = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=adaboost_tune,
    param_grid=param_grid_adaboost,
    scoring='roc_auc', # Optimize for ROC AUC
    cv=3, # Use 3-fold cross-validation
    n_jobs=-1, # Use all available cores
    verbose=2 # Display progress
)

# Fit GridSearchCV to the training data
grid_search_adaboost.fit(X_train, y_train)

print("GridSearchCV for AdaBoost Classifier completed successfully.")
print(f"Best parameters found: {grid_search_adaboost.best_params_}")
print(f"Best ROC AUC score: {grid_search_adaboost.best_score_:.4f}")

Fitting 3 folds for each of 9 candidates, totalling 27 fits
GridSearchCV for AdaBoost Classifier completed successfully.
Best parameters found: {'learning_rate': 1.0, 'n_estimators': 200}
Best ROC AUC score: 0.8451

Evaluate Tuned AdaBoost Classifier¶

In [109]:

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Get the best model from GridSearchCV
best_adaboost = grid_search_adaboost.best_estimator_

# Make predictions on the validation set using the best model
y_pred_adaboost_tuned = best_adaboost.predict(X_val)
y_pred_proba_adaboost_tuned = best_adaboost.predict_proba(X_val)[:, 1]

# Confusion Matrix

cm_adaboost_tuned = confusion_matrix(y_val, y_pred_adaboost_tuned)

# ROC AUC Score
roc_auc_adaboost_tuned = roc_auc_score(y_val, y_pred_proba_adaboost_tuned)
print(f"\nROC AUC Score (Tuned AdaBoost Classifier - Validation Set): {roc_auc_adaboost_tuned:.4f}")
