Hyperparameter Tuning for XGBoost Classifier¶

In [110]:
from xgboost import XGBClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

# Define the parameter grid to search
param_grid_xgb = {
    'n_estimators': [50, 100, 200], # Number of boosting rounds
    'learning_rate': [0.01, 0.1, 0.2], # Step size shrinkage
    'max_depth': [3, 5, 7] # Maximum depth of a tree
}

# Initialize XGBoost Classifier
xgb_tune = XGBClassifier(random_state=42, use_label_encoder=False, eval_metric='logloss')

# Initialize GridSearchCV
grid_search_xgb = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=xgb_tune,
    param_grid=param_grid_xgb,
    scoring='roc_auc', # Optimize for ROC AUC
    cv=3, # Use 3-fold cross-validation
    n_jobs=-1, # Use all available cores
    verbose=2 # Display progress
)

# Fit GridSearchCV to the training data
grid_search_xgb.fit(X_train, y_train)

print("GridSearchCV for XGBoost Classifier completed successfully.")
print(f"Best parameters found: {grid_search_xgb.best_params_}")
print(f"Best ROC AUC score: {grid_search_xgb.best_score_:.4f}")

Fitting 3 folds for each of 27 candidates, totalling 81 fits
GridSearchCV for XGBoost Classifier completed successfully.
Best parameters found: {'learning_rate': 0.1, 'max_depth': 3, 'n_estimators': 50}
Best ROC AUC score: 0.8481

Hyperparameter Tuning for Random Forest Classifier¶

In [112]:
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

# Define the parameter grid to search
param_grid_rf = {
    'n_estimators': [100, 200], # Number of trees in the forest
    'max_depth': [5, 10, None], # Maximum depth of the tree (None means unlimited)
    'min_samples_split': [2, 5] # Minimum number of samples required to split an internal node
}

# Initialize Random Forest Classifier
rf_tune = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=42)

# Initialize GridSearchCV
grid_search_rf = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=rf_tune,
    param_grid=param_grid_rf,
    scoring='roc_auc', # Optimize for ROC AUC
    cv=3, # Use 3-fold cross-validation
    n_jobs=-1, # Use all available cores
    verbose=2 # Display progress
)

# Fit GridSearchCV to the training data
grid_search_rf.fit(X_train, y_train)

print("GridSearchCV for Random Forest Classifier completed successfully.")
print(f"Best parameters found: {grid_search_rf.best_params_}")
print(f"Best ROC AUC score: {grid_search_rf.best_score_:.4f}")

Fitting 3 folds for each of 12 candidates, totalling 36 fits
GridSearchCV for Random Forest Classifier completed successfully.
Best parameters found: {'max_depth': 10, 'min_samples_split': 5, 'n_estimators': 200}
Best ROC AUC score: 0.8465

Evaluate Tuned Random Forest Classifier¶

In [113]:
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, roc_auc_score, confusion_matrix, ConfusionMatrixDisplay
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Get the best model from GridSearchCV
best_rf = grid_search_rf.best_estimator_

# Make predictions on the validation set using the best model
y_pred_rf_tuned = best_rf.predict(X_val)
y_pred_proba_rf_tuned = best_rf.predict_proba(X_val)[:, 1]

# Confusion Matrix
print("\nConfusion Matrix (Tuned Random Forest Classifier - Validation Set):")
cm_rf_tuned = confusion_matrix(y_val, y_pred_rf_tuned)
cmd_rf_tuned = ConfusionMatrixDisplay(confusion_matrix=cm_rf_tuned, display_labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
cmd_rf_tuned.plot(cmap='viridis')
plt.title('Confusion Matrix (Tuned Random Forest Classifier - Validation Set)')
plt.show()

# Classification Report (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score)
print("\nClassification Report (Tuned Random Forest Classifier - Validation Set):")
print(classification_report(y_val, y_pred_rf_tuned, target_names=['No Churn', 'Churn']))

# ROC AUC Score
roc_auc_rf_tuned = roc_auc_score(y_val, y_pred_proba_rf_tuned)
print(f"\nROC AUC Score (Tuned Random Forest Classifier - Validation Set): {roc_auc_rf_tuned:.4f}")

       Churn       0.72      0.58      0.64       510

Tuned Model Performance Comparison¶

Let's compare the performance of all the tuned models on the validation set using our chosen evaluation metrics.

In [114]:
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score, roc_auc_score
import pandas as pd

# Dictionary to store results for each tuned model
tuned_results = {
    'Model': [],
    'Accuracy': [],
    'Precision (Churn)': [],
    'Recall (Churn)': [],
    'F1-Score (Churn)': [],
    'ROC AUC': []
}

def add_tuned_model_results(model_name, y_true, y_pred, y_proba):
    tuned_results['Model'].append(model_name)
    tuned_results['Accuracy'].append(accuracy_score(y_true, y_pred))
    tuned_results['Precision (Churn)'].append(precision_score(y_true, y_pred, pos_label=1))
    tuned_results['Recall (Churn)'].append(recall_score(y_true, y_pred, pos_label=1))
    tuned_results['F1-Score (Churn)'].append(f1_score(y_true, y_pred, pos_label=1))
    tuned_results['ROC AUC'].append(roc_auc_score(y_true, y_proba))

# Add results for Tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier
add_tuned_model_results('Tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier', y_val, y_pred_gbc_tuned, y_pred_proba_gbc_tuned)

# Add results for Tuned Logistic Regression
add_tuned_model_results('Tuned Logistic Regression', y_val, y_pred_lr_tuned, y_pred_proba_lr_tuned)

# Add results for Tuned AdaBoost Classifier
add_tuned_model_results('Tuned AdaBoost Classifier', y_val, y_pred_adaboost_tuned, y_pred_proba_adaboost_tuned)

# Add results for Tuned XGBoost Classifier
add_tuned_model_results('Tuned XGBoost Classifier', y_val, y_pred_xgb_tuned, y_pred_proba_xgb_tuned)

# Add results for Tuned Random Forest Classifier
add_tuned_model_results('Tuned Random Forest Classifier', y_val, y_pred_rf_tuned, y_pred_proba_rf_tuned)

# Create DataFrame
tuned_results_df = pd.DataFrame(tuned_results)
tuned_results_df = tuned_results_df.round(4)

# Display the results table, sorted by ROC AUC
display(tuned_results_df.sort_values(by='ROC AUC', ascending=False))
Model Accuracy Precision (Churn) Recall (Churn) F1-Score (Churn) ROC AUC
0 Tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier 0.8225 0.7311 0.5863 0.6507 0.8634
3 Tuned XGBoost Classifier 0.8225 0.7392 0.5725 0.6453 0.8620
1 Tuned Logistic Regression 0.8202 0.7156 0.6020 0.6539 0.8618
4 Tuned Random Forest Classifier 0.8180 0.7191 0.5824 0.6436 0.8604
2 Tuned AdaBoost Classifier 0.8081 0.6918 0.5765 0.6289 0.8567
In [115]:
all_results_df = pd.concat([results_df, tuned_results_df], ignore_index=True)
all_results_df_sorted = all_results_df.sort_values(by='ROC AUC', ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True)
display(all_results_df_sorted)
Model Accuracy Precision (Churn) Recall (Churn) F1-Score (Churn) ROC AUC
0 Gradient Boosting Classifier 0.8213 0.7210 0.5980 0.6538 0.8647
1 Logistic Regression 0.8236 0.7195 0.6137 0.6624 0.8638
2 Tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier 0.8225 0.7311 0.5863 0.6507 0.8634
3 Tuned XGBoost Classifier 0.8225 0.7392 0.5725 0.6453 0.8620
4 Tuned Logistic Regression 0.8202 0.7156 0.6020 0.6539 0.8618
5 Tuned Random Forest Classifier 0.8180 0.7191 0.5824 0.6436 0.8604
6 AdaBoost Classifier 0.8059 0.6845 0.5784 0.6270 0.8569
7 Tuned AdaBoost Classifier 0.8081 0.6918 0.5765 0.6289 0.8567
8 XGBoost Classifier 0.8042 0.6789 0.5804 0.6258 0.8509
9 Random Forest Classifier 0.8103 0.6956 0.5824 0.6339 0.8479
10 Bagging Classifier 0.7942 0.6708 0.5314 0.5930 0.8232
11 K-Nearest Neighbors 0.7572 0.5697 0.5686 0.5692 0.7875
12 Decision Tree 0.7329 0.5242 0.5725 0.5473 0.6842

Overall Best Model¶

Based on the combined results of both untuned and tuned models, sorted by ROC AUC:

  • The untuned Gradient Boosting Classifier stands out as the best performing model with the highest ROC AUC score of 0.8647.
  • It also demonstrates a strong balance across other critical metrics for the 'Churn' class:
    • Precision (Churn): 0.7200
    • Recall (Churn): 0.6000
    • F1-Score (Churn): 0.6500

Although hyperparameter tuning was performed, in this specific instance, the default parameters of the Gradient Boosting Classifier yielded slightly better results on the validation set than its tuned counterpart. The untuned Gradient Boosting Classifier is the best model for this churn prediction task.

Best Model Performance On Test Set¶

In [116]:
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, roc_auc_score, confusion_matrix, ConfusionMatrixDisplay
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Re-initialize the untuned Gradient Boosting Classifier (as it was identified as the best)
# Using the default parameters as used in the initial comparison
best_final_model = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=42)
best_final_model.fit(X_train_val, y_train_val) # Train on combined training and validation data

# Make predictions on the test set
y_pred_test = best_final_model.predict(X_test)
y_pred_proba_test = best_final_model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

# Confusion Matrix
print("\nConfusion Matrix (Best Model - Test Set):")
cm_test = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred_test)
cmd_test = ConfusionMatrixDisplay(confusion_matrix=cm_test, display_labels=['No Churn', 'Churn'])
cmd_test.plot(cmap='viridis')
plt.title('Confusion Matrix (Best Model - Test Set)')
plt.show()

# Classification Report (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score)
print("\nClassification Report (Best Model - Test Set):")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred_test, target_names=['No Churn', 'Churn']))

# ROC AUC Score
roc_auc_test = roc_auc_score(y_test, y_pred_proba_test)
print(f"\nROC AUC Score (Best Model - Test Set): {roc_auc_test:.4f}")

Confusion Matrix (Best Model - Test Set):

Classification Report (Best Model - Test Set):
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

    No Churn       0.85      0.83      0.84      1297
       Churn       0.59      0.62      0.61       511

    accuracy                           0.77      1808
   macro avg       0.72      0.73      0.72      1808
weighted avg       0.78      0.77      0.77      1808


ROC AUC Score (Best Model - Test Set): 0.8056

Test Set Performance¶

The evaluation of the untuned Gradient Boosting Classifier on the test set reveals the following:

  • Accuracy: The model achieved an accuracy of approximately 0.77, indicating it correctly classified about 77% of the customers in the test set.

  • Precision (Churn): The precision for the 'Churn' class is around 0.59. This means that when the model predicts a customer will churn, it is correct approximately 59% of the time.

  • Recall (Churn): The recall for the 'Churn' class is approximately 0.62. This indicates that the model successfully identified about 62% of the actual churners in the test set.

  • F1-Score (Churn): The F1-score for the 'Churn' class is approximately 0.61. This score provides a balanced measure of precision and recall, reflecting a solid performance in identifying churners.

  • ROC AUC Score: The ROC AUC score on the test set is approximately 0.8056. This value signifies that the model has a strong ability to distinguish between churners and non-churners across various classification thresholds.

Overall, the model's ability to identify actual churners (recall) is strong, which is a key objective for this task.

Actionable Insights & Recommendations¶

Key Insights from Analysis¶

  1. Contract Type Matters: Month-to-month contracts are associated with the highest churn rate (approx. 46%), while longer-term contracts (one-year and two-year) drastically reduce churn (11.3% and 3.3% respectively).
  2. Early Churn is Critical: A large proportion of churn occurs within the first 12 months of service, indicating a potential issue with initial customer experience or onboarding.
  3. Fiber Optic Service and Churn: Customers using Fiber Optic internet service exhibit a significantly higher churn rate (approx. 44.9%) compared to DSL users or those without internet service. This suggests potential dissatisfaction with Fiber Optic service quality, pricing, or support.
  4. Payment Method Impact: Customers using electronic checks have the highest churn rate (approx. 49.1%), whereas automatic payment methods (bank transfer and credit card) are linked to much lower churn.
  5. Internet Security Features Drive Retention: The absence of online security, online backup, device protection, or tech support features correlates with considerably higher churn rates. Customers who opt for these features are notably more loyal.

Actionable Business Recommendations¶

  1. Strategize for Longer-Term Contracts: Actively encourage customers to switch from month-to-month to one-year or two-year contracts by offering significant incentives such as reduced monthly rates, free upgrades, or waived fees for committing to longer terms.
  2. Prioritize New Customer Retention Programs: Implement targeted onboarding programs and proactive outreach for new customers (especially within their first year) to address potential issues early and foster engagement through introductory discounts or dedicated support channels.
  3. Investigate and Improve Fiber Optic Service: Conduct in-depth analysis into the root causes of high churn among Fiber Optic users. Survey these customers, review service outage reports, and consider service enhancements or competitive pricing adjustments.
  4. Optimize Payment Method Options: Analyze issues associated with electronic checks and promote automatic payment methods. Incentivize customers (e.g., with small monthly discounts) to switch to automatic bank transfers or credit card payments, emphasizing convenience.
  5. Promote and Incentivize Security and Support Services: Highlight the benefits of Online Security, Online Backup, Device Protection, and Tech Support. Bundle these services into attractive packages, offer free trials, or provide discounts for adoption to reduce churn and increase customer stickiness.
